events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Replacing provisional governors

events.title: Extending the Freedmen's Bureau
events.description: Johnson vetoes a bill calling for the extension of the Freedmen's Bureau. The bill, a response to the repressive Black Codes of the South, would expand the power of the Bureau, the organization formed for the freedmen's protection.
events.impact: Aided formerly enslaved people

events.title: Johnson vetoes the Civil Rights Act
events.description: Johnson vetoes the Civil Rights Act, a second attempt by Congress to provide freedmen with federal citizenship after the failed Freedmen's Bureau bill. The act sanctions the employment of federal troops for enforcement. The Senate overrides Johnson's veto on April 6. Three days later, the House of Representatives also overrides the veto.
events.impact: Aided formerly enslaved people

events.title: William Dennison, James Speed, and James Harlan resign
events.description: William Dennison, James Speed, and James Harlan, all possessing ties to the “Radical Republicans,” resign from the cabinet. President Johnson replaces them with Republicans who support his policies.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William Dennison, James Speed, and James Harlan resign

events.title: Congress overrides Johnson's veto
events.description: Congress overrides Johnson's veto of the Freedmen's Bureau Renewal Act.
events.impact: Aided formerly enslaved people

events.title: Johnson begins speaking tour
events.description: Johnson begins his “swing around the circle” speaking tour of the eastern and midwestern United States. Hoping to gather popular support, he campaigns against several “Radical Republicans” running in the fall congressional elections. He returns to Washington, D.C., on September 15.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson begins speaking tour

events.title: First Reconstruction Act
events.description: With the mandate of the 1866 election, Congress (despite Johnson's veto) passes the First Reconstruction Act, setting up five military districts in the South, each under the direction of a presidentially-appointed military commander. The legislative body also passed the Army Appropriations Act, which lessens Johnson's control of the Army. Finally, Congress passes -- again over Johnson's veto -- the Tenure of Office Act, prohibiting Johnson from removing cabinet officers without the Senate's consent. In this final piece of legislation, Congress hopes to protect Secretary of War Edwin Stanton, the sole Radical Republican in Johnson's cabinet.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Reconstruction Act

events.title: Johnson vetoes Second Reconstruction Act, overridden
events.description: Johnson vetoes the Second Reconstruction Act, which orders military commanders to call elections in the South. Congress overrides Johnson's veto that very day.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson vetoes Second Reconstruction Act, overridden

events.title: Johnson vetoes Third Reconstruction Act, overridden
events.description: Johnson vetoes the Third Reconstruction Act, which spells out election procedures in the South and reasserts congressional control over Reconstruction. Congress again overrides Johnson's veto on the same day the President delivers it.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson vetoes Third Reconstruction Act, overridden

events.title: Johnson asks Secretary of War to resign
events.description: Johnson asks Secretary of War (and Radical Republican) Edwin Stanton to resign. The two disagree over Reconstruction plans; Stanton refuses.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson asks Secretary of War to resign

events.title: Johnson suspends Secretary Stanton
events.description: Johnson suspends Secretary Stanton from his position and commissions Ulysses S. Grant as ad interim secretary of war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson suspends Secretary Stanton

events.title: Johnson gives annual message
events.description: In his annual message to Congress, Johnson defends his policies toward the ex-Confederate states.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Johnson gives annual message

events.title: Defending Stanton's suspension
events.description: Johnson submits his reasons for suspending Secretary Stanton to the Senate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Defending Stanton's suspension

events.title: Johnson violates Tenure of Office Act
events.description: Johnson formally removes Stanton and gives control of the War Department to General Lorenzo Thomas. Stanton, however, refuses to adhere to Johnson's decision and barricades himself in his cabinet office for roughly two months. Johnson's actions violate the Tenure of Office Act and begin the impeachment crisis.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson violates Tenure of Office Act

events.title: President Johnson vetoes readmission, overridden
events.description: President Johnson vetoes bills that would have readmitted several ex-Confederate states to the Union. Congress overrides these vetoes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Johnson vetoes readmission, overridden

events.title: Burlingame Treaty
events.description: Johnson submits the Burlingame Treaty between the United States and China to the Senate for approval.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Burlingame Treaty

events.title: Johnson's final message
events.description: President Johnson delivers his final annual message to Congress, again requesting the repeal of the Reconstruction Acts.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Johnson's final message

events.title: Ulysses S. Grant inaugurated
events.description: Grant takes the oath of office as President of the United States. President Johnson refuses to attend the inauguration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ulysses S. Grant inaugurated

events.title: Ulysses S. Grant inaugurated
events.description: Grant, the Civil War hero and Republican candidate, is inaugurated as the eighteenth President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ulysses S. Grant inaugurated

events.title: Grant vetoes the Private Relief Bill
events.description: Grant vetoes the Private Relief Bill and will continue to veto many additional relief bills during his two terms.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grant vetoes the Private Relief Bill

events.title: Fenian Army of Vermont
events.description: Grant issues a proclamation against the attempts of the Fenian Brotherhood to damage Anglo-United States relations by attacking Canada. The next day, the Fenian Army of Vermont attempts to invade Canada but is driven back. The British government agrees to handle compensation for Canada in the Treaty of Washington in 1871, which will be signed by both nations on May 8, 1871.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: First civil service commission
events.description: Grant establishes the first civil service commission. Without additional appropriations from Congress, however, the commission is rendered ineffective.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First civil service commission

events.title: The KKK in South Carolina
events.description: Grant issues a proclamation against the Ku Klux Klan in South Carolina.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The KKK in South Carolina

events.title: Interoceanic Canal Commission
events.description: Grant appoints a special Interoceanic Canal Commission to determine the best of three proposed canal plans connecting the Atlantic with the Pacific. In February 1876, the commission reports in favor of a route through Nicaragua.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Interoceanic Canal Commission

events.title: RNC nominates Grant for reelection
events.description: The Republican National Convention nominates Grant for reelection and Senator Henry Wilson for vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC nominates Grant for reelection

events.title: Grant reelected
events.description: Grant is reelected in the largest popular-majority victory for a Republican in the nineteenth century. He wins 55.6 percent of the popular vote and 214 electoral votes to Greeley's 80. The result is more an expression of dislike for Greeley than support for Grant.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grant reelected

events.title: Grant inaugurated
events.description: Grant is inaugurated for his second term as President, and Vice President Henry Wilson is sworn in.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grant inaugurated

events.title: Grant vetoes inflation bill
events.description: After vacillating on the issue, Grant vetoes the inflation bill, passed by Congress, which would have increased the money supply by $100 million to alleviate the effects of the depression. Grant understands the motivation behind the measure but believes that inflating the currency is a dangerous strategy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grant vetoes inflation bill

events.title: "White League" of Louisiana
events.description: Grant issues a presidential proclamation calling for the dispersal of the rebellious “White League” in Louisiana. Grant sends five thousand troops and three gunboats to New Orleans; the resistance ends two days later. Grant and the Republicans are criticized severely for the intervention.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "White League" of Louisiana

events.title: Specie Resumption Act
events.description: The Specie Resumption Act is passed, allowing fractional currency and legal-tender notes to be redeemed for coin, beginning January 1, 1879. Sponsored by John Sherman, the bill also increases the number of national banks throughout the country. Grant sends a special message to Congress approving the bill.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Specie Resumption Act

events.title: Civil Rights Act of 1875
events.description: Grant signs the Civil Rights Act of 1875, guaranteeing black Americans equal rights in public places and prohibiting their exclusion from jury duty. The act includes no enforcement provisions and will be declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in 1883. School integration, championed by the now-deceased Charles Sumner, is not included in the bill. The act, nevertheless, creates an important precedent.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Civil Rights Act of 1875

events.title: Rutherford B. Hayes inaugurated
events.description: Republican Rutherford B. Hayes, previously a Union soldier, as well as a representative and governor of Ohio, is publicly sworn in as the nineteenth President of the United States. He takes the oath privately on March 3rd. In the race against Democrat Samuel Tilden, Hayes secures only 48 percent of the popular vote and 164 electoral votes to Tilden's 184. However, voter fraud and unclear results are reported in several states. A Special Electoral Commission is formed with members of the US Senate, House of Representatives, and the Supreme Court. It issues a controversial decision that proclaims Hayes as President, with some Democrats referring to Hayes as “Rutherfraud.” The Disputed Election of 1876
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Rutherford B. Hayes inaugurated

events.title: North and South relations
events.description: At a cabinet meeting, Hayes agrees to send a commission to Louisiana to report on the conditions in the southern state. In reality, he plans to use the action to sanction his decision to allow Democrat Francis T. Nicholls to take over the states by removing federal aid from federally appointed governor Stephen B. Meanwhile, Hayes's cabinet includes staunch liberal Republican William Evarts as Secretary of State and a former Confederate as postmaster general; the nomination of the latter appeases Southern Democrats as part of the Compromise of 1877.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: North and South relations

events.title: Patrolling Mexican border
events.description: With Mexican-Texas border incursions continuing, Hayes sends troops to patrol the nearly lawless Mexican border and cross it if necessary to pursue bandits. Mexican president Diaz protests and sends troops to the border as well. Ultimately, economic concerns motivate both parties to work towards a settlement.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Patrolling Mexican border

events.title: Civil service reform
events.description: Following John Jay's investigation of the New York Customhouse, Hayes issues an Executive Order that forbids the involvement of federal employees in political activities. The President takes such action in the hope that it will curtail corruption; the Executive Order stipulates that those in office can no longer be dismissed for political reasons. Congress rejects additional proposals. These events testify to Hayes's interest in civil service reform.
events.impact: Government corruption

events.title: Great Railroad Strike of 1877
events.description: Following pay cuts, the first major interstate strike -- the Great Railroad Strike of 1877 -- begins on the Baltimore and Ohio (B&O) line at Camden Junction, Maryland; additional strikes will follow, lasting a month. Lacking organization, the strikes frequently degenerate into mob activity. Hayes sends federal troops to protect mail and quell the riots that take place in numerous cities, angering many workers. The strike will lead to anti-Chinese attacks in San Francisco during the fall.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Great Railroad Strike of 1877

events.title: Hayes tours the South
events.description: Hayes goes on a tour of the South, pledging reconciliation and solidarity through a policy of pacification.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes tours the South

events.title: Hayes versus Conkling
events.description: Hayes challenges the political power of New York Senator Roscoe Conkling when the President announces he will replace Collector of the Port of New York Chester A. Arthur, as well as naval officer Alonzo Cornell, during the reorganization of the New York Customhouse. Although Arthur has overseen improvement in the Customhouse, he also uses it to further Conkling's political interests. Incensed by Hayes's decision, Conkling, Hayes's opponent for the Republican presidential ticket in 1876, blocks Hayes's nomination of Theodore Roosevelt, Sr., for the position.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes versus Conkling

events.title: Hayes vetoes Bland-Allison Act, overridden
events.description: Hayes vetoes the Bland-Allison Act, advocated by farmers and debtors, but Congress passes the measure over his veto. The act calls for the resumption of silver coinage at a rate between $2 and $4 million per month.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes vetoes Bland-Allison Act, overridden

events.title: Hayes limits Chinese immigration
events.description: Hayes vetoes a bill which bans incoming vessels from carrying more than fifteen Chinese passengers. Hayes then works to negotiate changes to the Burlingame Treaty with China in order to set limits on Chinese immigration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes limits Chinese immigration

events.title: Army Appropriations Bill
events.description: Congress passes the Army Appropriations Bill. The law includes a “rider” which forbids the use of federal troops at polls, which many regard as an attempt to nullify black voting rights. Hayes vetoes the bill, but the House sustains the veto. Hayes again vetoes the rebuffed version, and many Republicans feel the veto secures the election of 1880.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Army Appropriations Bill

events.title: Hayes vetoes appropriations bill
events.description: Hayes vetoes a version of the appropriations bill for the third time; a later bill excludes “certain judicial expenses” forbidding the army to “police the polls”; Hayes will agree to this language.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes vetoes appropriations bill

events.title: Hayes vetoes appropriations
events.description: The appropriations designated by Democrats exclude implementation of election law funds; Hayes vetoes the bill.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes vetoes appropriations

events.title: Hayes supports a canal
events.description: In a speech to Congress, Hayes continues to support a Central American canal to unite the Atlantic and Pacific oceans. Following the trip to America by French diplomat Ferdinand de Lesseps -- the builder of the Suez Canal in Egypt -- Hayes states that “the policy of this country is a canal under American control.” A canal running through Panama will be completed in 1914.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Hayes supports a canal

events.title: Garfield Inaugurated
events.description: A self-made man, Republican James A. Garfield is sworn in as the twentieth President of the United States. Garfield served in the Union Army and in the House of Representatives before running for President. Winning the popular election by an very small margin -- only 10,000 votes -- Garfield owes his success partially to New York political boss and Senator William Conkling. In return, Garfield pledges to consult Conkling on government nominations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield Inaugurated

events.title: Garfield selects his cabinet
events.description: Garfield completes his slate of cabinet members, naming James G. Blaine as Secretary of State and Abraham Lincoln's son, Robert, as Secretary of War. Garfield angers Conkling with his nomination of William Windom of Minnesota, a non-Eastern man, as Secretary of Treasury. Further, Garfield denies Conkling influence in New York politics by appointing William H. Robertson as collector of the port of New York and Thomas L. James postmaster of New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield selects his cabinet

events.title: Garfield sends nominations to Senate
events.description: Garfield sends his list of nominations to the Senate, which includes New York senator Conkling's contingent. Conkling will continue to be a source of conflict for the President.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield sends nominations to Senate

events.title: Garfield removes E. A. Meritt
events.description: Garfield removes E. Meritt from the collectorship of the New York Customhouse after Conkling feels assured that the President would not make any such changes. Garfield then sends W. Robertson's name to the Senate as his replacement, intensifying the struggle between Garfield and Conkling.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield removes E. A. Meritt

events.title: Filibuster begins
events.description: A Democratic filibuster, which ties up the Senate beginning March 23, ends when Garfield agrees to remove certain appointments. The end of the filibuster allows Garfield to push for Robertson's confirmation to the New York Customhouse. Earlier, Senator Conkling threatens to publish the Hubbell letter, which appears to link Garfield to the Star Route Scandal, a scheme to skim money from the U.S. Post Office. The link is not a significant one, however, and the publication of the letter proves more damaging to Conkling than Garfield.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Filibuster begins

events.title: Garfield removes nominations
events.description: On the eve of the senatorial vote on the New York nominees, Garfield learns that Conkling intends to delay action on other nominees and moves for adjournment before Robertson can be considered. Garfield removes all of his nominations with the exception of Robertson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield removes nominations

events.title: Garfield Shot
events.description: President Garfield is shot in a D.C. train station by Charles Julius Guiteau, an unstable Stalwart attorney who had been denied a consular post. “I am a stalwart,” Guiteau proclaims. “Arthur is now President of the United States.” President James Garfield Shot On July 2, 1881, President James Garfield was shot by Charles J. Guiteau as he walked through the Baltimore & Potomac Railroad station with Secretary of State James Blaine. Wounded after only four months in office, the President died from his wounds on September 19, 1881, and Vice President Chester A. Arthur was sworn in as President. President Garfield had never paid a great deal of attention to securing himself from possible assassination, likening the possibility of the event to the risk of being struck by lightning-all but impossible to prevent and thus pointless to worry about. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield Shot

events.title: Garfield Dies
events.description: James Garfield dies from blood poisoning and complications after surgeons search endlessly to find the lost bullet in his back, lodged in his pancreas. Vice President Chester A. Arthur becomes the twenty-first President of the United States The assassin, Guiteau, will be hanged on June 30, 1882.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield Dies

events.title: Arthur Becomes Vice President
events.description: Arthur takes office as vice president in the presidential administration of James Garfield.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur Becomes Vice President

events.title: Arthur Becomes President
events.description: Arthur is sworn in as President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur Becomes President

events.title: Arthur Inaugurated
events.description: Arthur formally takes the oath of office in Washington, DC.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur Inaugurated

events.title: Chinese Exclusion Act vetoed
events.description: Arthur vetoes the first Chinese Exclusion Act, which would have banned the immigration of Chinese laborers for twenty years and denied American citizenship to current Chinese residents; the veto greatly angers labor groups, who feel increasingly threatened by the influx of Chinese labor.
events.impact: Banned immigration based on race

events.title: Arthur appoints Tariff Commission
events.description: Arthur approves a bill to appoint a tariff commission; the commission eventually recommends tariff reductions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur appoints Tariff Commission

events.title: Steamboat Safety Bill vetoed
events.description: Arthur vetoes the Carriage of Passengers at Sea Bill, a steamboat safety bill, claiming that it contains several major technical errors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Steamboat Safety Bill vetoed

events.title: River and Harbor Act vetoed
events.description: The President vetoes the River and Harbor Act, a pork-barrel piece of legislation that Arthur claimed would benefit only “particular localities;” Congress overrides the veto and passes the legislation the next day.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: River and Harbor Act vetoed

events.title: Arthur increases Naval funds
events.description: Recognizing the disgraceful state of the U.S. Navy, Arthur signs a bill appropriating funds for the Navy's first steel vessels.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur increases Naval funds

events.title: Korean Ambassadors visit
events.description: Arthur receives Korean ambassadors in New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Korean Ambassadors visit

events.title: Arthur celebrates Washington
events.description: Arthur issues a proclamation recommending the observance of the 100th anniversary of General George Washington returning his commission as commander-in-chief to the Continental Congress.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur celebrates Washington

events.title: Arthur requests funds for Navy
events.description: In a special message to Congress, Arthur asks the legislature to appropriate funds for naval reconstruction work.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur requests funds for Navy

events.title: Proclamation against settlement in Oklahoma
events.description: Arthur issues a proclamation warning people not to settle on Oklahoma lands.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Proclamation against settlement in Oklahoma

events.title: Cleveland Inaugurated
events.description: Grover Cleveland is inaugurated as the twenty-second President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland Inaugurated

events.title: Grover Cleveland inaugurated
events.description: Former New York Governor Grover Cleveland is sworn in and takes office as the twenty-second President of the United States. Cleveland is the nation's first Democratic President since James Buchanan, who served prior to the onset of the Civil War. In his inaugural address, Cleveland promises to adhere to “business principals,” indicative of his conservative outlook.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grover Cleveland inaugurated

events.title: Cleveland Signs Presidential Succession Act
events.description: On January 19, 1886, President Grover Cleveland signed the Presidential Succession Act. The act specified that in the absence of a President and vice president, heads of executive departments would succeed to the presidency in the order in which the departments were created, starting with the secretary of state. The Presidential Succession Act of 1886 remained in force until 1947. Vice President Thomas Hendricks died in his Indianapolis home in November 1885. President Grover Cleveland's message to Congress on December 8 regarding the death of the new vice president called for a constitutional amendment to clarify the line of succession should both the President and vice president die or become unable to serve. While the Presidential Succession Act that was proposed and passed shortly afterward on January 15, 1886, was primarily the work of the Congress, President Cleveland supported the legislation and signed the bill into law. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland Signs Presidential Succession Act

events.title: Cleveland addresses Congress
events.description: In a message to Congress, Cleveland asserts that labor is a vital element of national prosperity and should be a concern of the federal government. He suggests the creation of a government committee to resolve disputes between labor and capital, making him the first President to do so.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland addresses Congress

events.title: Cleveland vetoes military pensions
events.description: Cleveland vetoes the first of several bills granting military pensions to Civil War Union veterans who had appealed to Congress after their claims were rejected by the Pensions Bureau. Hundreds of these claims are bogus.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland vetoes military pensions

events.title: Accepting the Statue of Liberty
events.description: Cleveland recommends to Congress that the nation accept France's gift of the Statue of Liberty. The gift commemorates the alliance between the two countries during the Revolutionary War. The statue will be placed on Liberty Island, adjacent to Ellis Island off the New Jersey coast. Ellis Island will serve as a welcoming center for the soaring number of immigrants to New York City.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Accepting the Statue of Liberty

events.title: Cleveland announces his engagement
events.description: Cleveland announces that he is to marry 21-year-old Frances Folsom, touching off a media frenzy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland announces his engagement

events.title: Interstate Commerce Commission
events.description: Following complaints about railroad rates and policies, the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) is created to ensure fairness in the management of interstate railroads. Eventually, the scope of the ICC will expand to include all common carriers. The commission is the nation's first independent regulatory agency. Although Cleveland approves its creation, he has reservations about the agency.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Interstate Commerce Commission

events.title: Cleveland Signs Dawes Act
events.description: On February 8, 1887, President Grover Cleveland signed the General Allotment Act into law. The law, commonly known as the Dawes Act after Senator Henry L. Dawes of Massachusetts who proposed it, divided tribal lands of Native Americans into individual allotments and encouraged the assimilation of Native Americans into American society. In 1881, Helen Hunt Jackson published A Century of Dishonor , detailing the federal government's mistreatment of American Indians. Her work created a strong general sentiment for reform of the government's policy toward Native Americans. Senator Dawes proposed the General Allotment Act in 1886 to attempt to put an end to the abuses described in Jackson's book. The bill gave 160 acres of land to each family, 80 acres to single adults and 40 acres to orphaned children, and prohibited recipients from selling the land for a period of 25 years. Indians who renounced their tribal holdings were made eligible for U.S. citizenship. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland Signs Dawes Act

events.title: Cleveland vetoes Dependent Pension Bill
events.description: Cleveland vetoes the Dependent Pension Bill, which would have given a military pension to anyone serving a minimum of ninety days in any war. He argues that the bill will only encourage fraudulent assertions.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Cleveland vetoes Dependent Pension Bill

events.title: Cleveland vetoes Texas Seed Bill
events.description: Cleveland vetoes the Texas Seed Bill, which was designed to provide relief to drought-stricken farmers. Cleveland believes the bill oversteps the powers of the federal government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland vetoes Texas Seed Bill

events.title: Tenure of Office Act of 1867
events.description: The Tenure of Office Act of 1867 is repealed after Cleveland challenges its constitutionality. The act had required that the President gain Senate approval to remove from office any individuals who had received Senate confirmation upon appointment. Congress had passed the bill in order gain control over President Andrew Johnson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tenure of Office Act of 1867

events.title: Cleveland tours South and Southwest
events.description: Cleveland embarks on a tour that takes him to states in the southern and western United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland tours South and Southwest

events.title: Cleveland addresses Congress
events.description: In his annual address to Congress, the President argues against protective tariffs, which he claims are creating an excessive surplus. High tariffs were adopted during the Civil War to protect American industrial interests as a temporary measure; they remained in force, however, after the war. Following Cleveland's message, Representative Roger Q. Mills, a Texas Democrat, introduces a moderate bill that reduces rates and favors the South. The Senate rejects this bill and tariff reform becomes one of the divisive issues of the 1888 presidential election.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland addresses Congress

events.title: Cleveland appoints Lucius Q.C. Lamar
events.description: Cleveland appoints Lucius Q.C. Lamar of Mississippi to the Supreme Court. Previously, Lamar serves as Cleveland's secretary of the interior.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland appoints Lucius Q.C. Lamar

events.title: Cleveland appoints Melville W. Fuller
events.description: Cleveland appoints Melville W. Fuller chief justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. During his second term, the President will come in for significant criticism from political opponents as he will be associated with the conservative decisions of the Fuller Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland appoints Melville W. Fuller

events.title: DNC nominates Cleveland
events.description: Cleveland accepts the Democratic nomination for President. Ex-Ohio senator Allen G. Thurman is the vice-presidential nominee.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: DNC nominates Cleveland

events.title: Cleveland Signs Chinese Exclusion Act
events.description: On October 8, 1888, President Grover Cleveland signed the Chinese Exclusion Act, which restricted Chinese immigration to the United States. The law prohibited Chinese immigrants who returned to China from coming back to the United States. President Chester Arthur passed the first bill limiting Chinese immigration in 1882, and the federal government did not eradicate barriers to Chinese immigration until 1943. During the 1880s, racial tension on the West Coast between whites and Chinese laborers put significant pressure on the U.S. government to place restrictions on Chinese immigration. This pressure resulted in an outright ban on immigrant Chinese laborers in the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882. But this ban was not enough to quell the growing anti-Chinese sentiment, which erupted in riots in Rock Springs, Wyoming, and Tacoma and Seattle, Washington in 1885. In 1887, Secretary of State Thomas F. [truncated]
events.impact: Banned immigration based on race

events.title: Cleveland loses to Harrison
events.description: Although Cleveland wins the popular vote in presidential election, he loses to Benjamin Harrison in the electoral vote, 233 to 168. On November 6, 1888, President Grover Cleveland was defeated in his bid for re-election by the Republican candidate, Benjamin Harrison. Although President Cleveland won the popular vote, Harrison won the Electoral College and thus the presidency. The Republicans entered the election of 1888 with a well-organized and effective campaign structure. Their candidate, Benjamin Harrison, was the grandson of President William Henry Harrison. He was a former Civil War general and Indiana senator who ran an energetic campaign and exhausted himself delivering more than eighty speeches in sixteen weeks. The Democratic campaign was far less organized, and President Cleveland put forth little effort. Much of the public speaking in the Democratic campaign was left to the nominee for vice president, Allen G. Thurman, who was in poor health. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland loses to Harrison

events.title: Department of Agriculture created
events.description: Cleveland signs a bill creating the Department of Agriculture.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Department of Agriculture created

events.title: North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Washington becomes states
events.description: Cleveland signs a bill that turns the territories of North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, and Washington into states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Washington becomes states

events.title: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated
events.description: Benjamin Harrison is inaugurated. In a prophetic statement, Frances Cleveland tells the White House staff that she and Grover will return in four years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated

events.title: Cleveland inaugurated
events.description: Cleveland is inaugurated, becoming the only President to serve two nonconsecutive terms.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland inaugurated

events.title: Cleveland withdraws Hawaiian annexation treaty
events.description: Cleveland withdraws the Hawaiian annexation treaty, signed just prior to his inauguration. He takes the advice of a special commissioner who reports that proponents of the annexation are sugar planters; the majority of the population opposes such action. Cleveland advocates the restoration of the queen but the provisional government rejects this idea.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland withdraws Hawaiian annexation treaty

events.title: Cleveland defends gold standard
events.description: Cleveland vows to defend the gold standard.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland defends gold standard

events.title: Special session
events.description: Cleveland calls a special session of Congress for August 7, with the intent of handling the economic crisis through tariff reform and the repeal of the silver-purchase law. Concerned about the low gold reserve, Cleveland wants to end the practice of issuing silver notes that can be redeemed in gold.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session

events.title: Sherman Silver Purchase Act
events.description: The Sherman Silver Purchase Act, implemented under President Harrison, is repealed. Cleveland supports this policy shift.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sherman Silver Purchase Act

events.title: Pullman railway strikes
events.description: Eugene Debs, president of the American Railway Union, organizes a strike by employees of the Pullman railway car company, beginning in Pullman, Illinois. Company workers find themselves forced to live in the company town where costs are higher than elsewhere. Additionally, George Pullman lowers wages, in light of the 1893 depression, but maintains rent and other charges. The strike spreads throughout the West and halts rail service, affecting twenty-seven states and territories. Following a recommendation by Attorney General Richard Olney, Cleveland sends federal troops to Chicago on July 3. Eventually, Debs and others are arrested, and the strike is broken.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Pullman railway strikes

events.title: Cleveland vetoes illiteracy ban
events.description: Cleveland vetoes a bill which would ban illiterate immigrants.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland vetoes illiteracy ban

events.title: William McKinley inaugurated
events.description: William McKinley is inaugurated as the twenty-fifth President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William McKinley inaugurated

events.title: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated
events.description: Republican Benjamin Harrison is inaugurated as the twenty-third President after losing the popular vote to Grover Cleveland. The Republicans hold small majorities in both houses of Congress, making this the first time since 1875 that Republicans control both Congress and the White House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated

events.title: Roosevelt visits the White House
events.description: Harrison invites Theodore Roosevelt to the White House and appoints him Civil Service Commissioner on May 7. Roosevelt, a reform Republican from New York, heads the department until 1895.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Roosevelt visits the White House

events.title: Harrison tours New England
events.description: Building on the work of President Arthur, Harrison tours New England and reveals plans for an expanded merchant marine and two-ocean Navy. Expansion of the Navy will be a distinguishing feature of Harrison's presidency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison tours New England

events.title: Harrison's first message to Congress
events.description: Harrison sends his first message to Congress. Among his recommendations are civil rights and civil service reform, naval legislation, improved conditions for railroad workers, and pensions for veterans.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Harrison's first message to Congress

events.title: Harrison nominates a Supreme Court justice
events.description: Harrison nominates David J. Brewer to the Supreme Court. The Senate approves the choice two weeks later.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison nominates a Supreme Court justice

events.title: Sherman Silver Purchase Act
events.description: Harrison signs into law the Sherman Silver Purchase Act after convincing free silver senators to compromise on the legislation. Support comes from farmers who argue that increased silver coinage will inflate the currency supply and raise prices, as well as from leaders of new western states with silver mines. The law permits the Treasury to buy 4.5 million ounces of silver each month, doubling the previous purchase amount. Nevertheless, the bill has little effect on the economy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sherman Silver Purchase Act

events.title: Harrison requests to ban lottery tickets
events.description: Harrison sends a special message to Congress in which he requests legislation to ban lottery ticket sales by mail.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison requests to ban lottery tickets

events.title: Harrison appoints Henry B. Brown
events.description: Harrison appoints Henry B. Brown to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison appoints Henry B. Brown

events.title: Circuit Courts of Appeals created
events.description: Harrison signs a historic measure creating nine Circuit Courts of Appeals. The new Courts are set up to relieve the demands on the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Circuit Courts of Appeals created

events.title: Harrison addresses Congress
events.description: In his annual message to Congress, Harrison denounces the Valparaiso attack as “savage, brutal, unprovoked.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison addresses Congress

events.title: Harrison nominates Stephen B. Elkins as Secretary of War
events.description: Harrison nominates Stephen B. Elkins as the new secretary of war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison nominates Stephen B. Elkins as Secretary of War

events.title: Special message to Congress
events.description: In a special message to Congress, Harrison asks that lawmakers take “appropriate action” regarding Chile.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special message to Congress

events.title: Harrison decides on second run
events.description: Harrison decides to run for re-election; party bosses oppose him.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison decides on second run

events.title: Silver mine strike turns violent
events.description: Silver miners at Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, go on a violent strike. Thirty men are killed as they fight non-union help. Harrison sends in federal troops to restore order.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Silver mine strike turns violent

events.title: Grover Cleveland wins the Presidency
events.description: Garnering 43 percent of the popular vote, Harrison is defeated by Grover Cleveland, who gains 46 percent in the presidential election. Cleveland receives 277 electoral votes to Harrison's 145.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grover Cleveland wins the Presidency

events.title: Queen Liliuokalani of Hawaii deposed
events.description: Queen Liliuokalani of Hawaii is deposed on January 17, with a provisional government being established under Sanford B. News of the revolt reaches Washington on January 29. Harrison responds by deploying 150 marines to Hawaii to protect the new government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Queen Liliuokalani of Hawaii deposed

events.title: Attempting to annex Hawaii
events.description: Harrison sends a treaty to the Senate requesting “full and complete” annexation of Hawaii. The Senate, intensely divided, refuses to act.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Attempting to annex Hawaii

events.title: Grover Cleveland inaugurated
events.description: Grover Cleveland is inaugurated as President, and Harrison returns to Indianapolis.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grover Cleveland inaugurated

events.title: William McKinley inaugurated
events.description: William McKinley is inaugurated as the twenty-fifth President of the United States. McKinley asserts: “The country is suffering from industrial disturbances from which speedy relief must be had. Our financial system needs some revision; our money is all good now, but its value must not further be threatened.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William McKinley inaugurated

events.title: Special session called
events.description: President McKinley calls Congress into a special session for the purpose of revising the tariff laws.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session called

events.title: Dingley Tariff Law
events.description: President McKinley signs the Dingley Tariff Law, which raises custom duties by an average of 57 percent. Although American industries no longer needed such heavy protection against foreign goods, the tariff was raised nonetheless; imported woolen products, for example, faced a 91 percent rate. Republicans associate the high tariff with national prosperity while Democrats and progressives will blame the tariff for causing subsequent price increases.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Dingley Tariff Law

events.title: First annual message to Congress
events.description: President McKinley's first annual message to Congress is read aloud. The President states that while the government of Spain should be given time to reform its behavior in Cuba, America would continue to devote significant diplomatic attention to the island. McKinley also reminds Americans to refrain from factionalism: “Questions of foreign policy, of revenue, the soundness of the currency, the inviolability of national obligations, the improvement of the public service, appeal to the individual conscience of every earnest citizen to whatever party he belongs or in whatever section of the country he may reside.”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: First annual message to Congress

events.title: Increasing defense funds
events.description: At President McKinley's behest, Congress votes a $50 million appropriation for national defense.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Increasing defense funds

events.title: Civil war in Cuba
events.description: President McKinley asks Congress for authority to “use armed force” in Cuba to end the civil war. Meanwhile, Spanish Prime Minister Sagasta makes a last-minute peace concession by offering the Cubans limited autonomy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Civil war in Cuba

events.title: Ordering a Cuban blockade
events.description: President McKinley orders a blockade of northern Cuban ports.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ordering a Cuban blockade

events.title: Volunteers for war
events.description: President McKinley calls for 125,000 volunteers to fight the war with Spain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Volunteers for war

events.title: Additional volunteers for war
events.description: President McKinley issues a new call for volunteers, asking for an additional 75,000. troop expedition of 2,500 men also sets sail for Manila, Philippines, from San Francisco, California.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Additional volunteers for war

events.title: War Revenue Act
events.description: Congress passes the War Revenue Act, which generated about $150 million of tax revenue a year from taxes levied on beer, tobacco, amusements, and some business transactions. President McKinley signs the bill on June 13.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: War Revenue Act

events.title: Annexing Hawaii
events.description: On July 7, 1898, President William McKinley signed a bill that annexed the Hawaiian Islands, making them part of the United States. American military victories in the Pacific during the Spanish-American War helped bring to a close almost a decade of uncertainty about the status of the Hawaiian Islands. Prominent American colonists had supported the overthrow of Hawaiian Queen Liliuokalani in early 1891, but Democratic and anti-imperialist Republican opposition to annexation blocked the island chain's incorporation into the United States. When William McKinley assumed the presidency in 1897, he reversed the policy of his predecessor, Democrat Grover Cleveland, and advocated Hawaiian annexation. McKinley, however, was unable to push a new annexation treaty through Congress that year. After American forces swung into action in the Pacific during the Spanish-American War, the island chain's strategic importance became apparent. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Annexing Hawaii

events.title: Negotiating with Spain
events.description: President McKinley appoints U.S. peace commissioners for negotiations with Spain; Secretary of State William R. Day will lead the delegation. McKinley asks Day to resign his office to assume the leadership of the peace commission. John Hay becomes secretary of state on September 30.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Negotiating with Spain

events.title: Dodge Commisison
events.description: President McKinley appoints the Dodge Commission to investigate the conduct of the War Department.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Dodge Commisison

events.title: McKinley's second annual message
events.description: President McKinley's second annual message is read to both Houses of Congress. He declares his intention to build an inter-oceanic canal through Nicaragua and discusses the merits of fighting the Spanish-American war: “Military service under a common flag and for a righteous cause has strengthened the national spirit and served to cement more closely than ever the fraternal bonds between every section of the country.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley's second annual message

events.title: First Philippine Commisison
events.description: President McKinley appoints Jacob Gould Schurman chairman of the first Philippine Commission.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Philippine Commisison

events.title: Retreat from civil service reform
events.description: President McKinley issues an executive order exempting between 3,000 to 4,000 positions from competitive civil service examinations. McKinley's order marks a retreat from President Grover Cleveland's more aggressive practices of civil service reform.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Retreat from civil service reform

events.title: McKinley's third message to Congress
events.description: President McKinley sends his third annual message to Congress. It focuses largely on foreign affairs, with McKinley calling for beefing up the U.S. Navy to benefit overseas commerce. With regard to the Philippines, McKinley affirms, “I shall use the authority vested in me by the Constitution and the statutes to uphold the sovereignty of the United States in those distant islands as in all other places where our flag rightfully floatsÖ.Aiming only at the public good, we cannot err. A right interpretation of the people's will and of duty cannot fail to insure wise measures for the welfare of the islands which have come under the authority of the United States, and inure to the common interest and lasting honor of our country.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley's third message to Congress

events.title: Gold Standard Act
events.description: President McKinley signs the Gold Standard Act, which fixes the standard of value for all money issued or coined by the United States. It marks a victory for the so-called “goldbugs” from the Northeast and urban Midwest who pushed for gold-backed currency to stabilize industrial investment. Likewise, it is a crushing defeat for the free silver forces from the South and West who advocated silver coinage as a way to flush the agricultural economy with more available currency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Gold Standard Act

events.title: Second Philippine Commission
events.description: President McKinley appoints a Second Philippine Commission, chaired by William Howard Taft.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Second Philippine Commission

events.title: McKinley formally accepts nomination
events.description: President McKinley formally accepts the Republican presidential nomination in a speech at Canton, Ohio. McKinley's campaign slogan reminded voters of previous prosperity and promised more of the same: “Four more years of the full dinner pail.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley formally accepts nomination

events.title: McKinley reelected
events.description: William McKinley is re-elected President of the United States, with Theodore Roosevelt elected as vice president. McKinley wins with 292 electoral votes against 155 for William Jennings Bryan; Social Democratic candidate Eugene V. Debs secures 94,768 popular votes. Republicans strengthen their hold on both houses of Congress, securing a 55-31 majority in the Senate and a 197-151 majority in the House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley reelected

events.title: McKinley's fourth message to Congress
events.description: President McKinley submits his fourth annual message to Congress. He notes that in foreign affairs, “the dominant question has been the treatment of the Chinese problem. Apart from this our relations with the powers have been happy.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley's fourth message to Congress

events.title: McKinley inaugurated
events.description: William McKinley is inaugurated as President for a second term, with Theodore Roosevelt sworn in as vice president. McKinley calls for the Filipino rebellion to end “without further bloodshed,” wising that “there be ushered in the reign of peace to be made permanent by a government of liberty under law!”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: McKinley inaugurated

events.title: McKinley declines to run again
events.description: President McKinley announces he will not be a candidate for a third term.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley declines to run again

events.title: Tariff reciprocity
events.description: Speaking in Buffalo, New York, President McKinley endorses the concept of tariff reciprocity. McKinley also notes, in what would be his last speech, “The period of exclusiveness is past. The expansion of our trade and commerce is the pressing problem.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tariff reciprocity

events.title: McKinley shot
events.description: Leon Czolgosz shoots McKinley in the stomach while the President shakes hands at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. Czolgosz, an anarchist, admitted to the shooting, and he expressed no remorse for his actions. He died in the electric chair on October 29, 1901.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley shot

events.title: McKinley dies
events.description: President McKinley dies from his wounds as the result of complications due to gangrene, and Vice President Theodore Roosevelt takes the oath of office to become the twenty-sixth President of the United States. President McKinley Dies On September 14, 1901, President William McKinley died, eight days after he was shot by an assassin. He had served only six months of his second presidential term before his death. After vacationing in Canton, Ohio, in the summer of 1901, President McKinley and his staff set off for the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. On September 5, McKinley delivered a speech to a crowd of nearly 50,000 on his policy goals for his second term; his particular focus was on the promotion of American foreign trade. The next afternoon, the President attended a public reception at the Exposition's Temple of Music, accompanied by three secret service bodyguards. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: McKinley dies

events.title: Roosevelt takes office
events.description: Vice President Theodore Roosevelt (TR) takes the oath of office in Buffalo, New York, after President William McKinley is assassinated. Roosevelt becomes the twenty-sixth President of the United States and the youngest President yet at 43 years old. Roosevelt's succession appalls Republicans who blanched at his liberal leanings; TR was nominated for the vice presidency in 1900 partly because Republican leaders were attempting to relegate him to a harmless position.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt takes office

events.title: Crater Lake National Park
events.description: The President establishes Crater Lake National Park in Oregon.
events.impact: Preserved natural lands for the public

events.title: Anthracite Coal Strike
events.description: TR plays a key role in settling the Anthracite Coal Strike. During the spring of 1902, laborers tied to the United Mine Workers union had walked off the job in the hard coal mines of Pennsylvania. The prospect of coal shortages in the winter months loomed, and TR decided that public interest demanded vigorous executive action. Roosevelt summoned union leaders and mine operators to the White House, a significant gesture for both his presidency and for the development of his reform program, known as the “Square Deal.” The coal strike ended on October 21.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Anthracite Coal Strike

events.title: Department of Commerce and Labor created
events.description: Roosevelt signs a bill creating the Department of Commerce and Labor, the ninth Cabinet office, which will itself emerge as two separate departments in 1913.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Department of Commerce and Labor created

events.title: First federal bird reservation
events.description: Roosevelt proclaims Pelican Island, Florida, as the first federal bird reservation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First federal bird reservation

events.title: Roosevelt elected
events.description: TR wins the presidential election, trouncing Democratic candidate Alton B. Parker, 336 electoral votes to 140. With the exception of Maryland, Roosevelt wins every state north of Washington, D.C., including all Midwestern and Western states; Parker sweeps the South and Texas. In the Senate, the Republicans maintain their 57 to 33 advantage, while in the House, they gain 43 seats, for a 250-136 majority. Roosevelt vows to not seek another presidential term in order to deflect Democratic charges that he would remain in office for life.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt elected

events.title: Roosevelt sends annual message to Congress
events.description: In his annual message to Congress, the President issues the “Roosevelt Corollary” to the Monroe Doctrine. Roosevelt augments the justification for U.S. intervention in the Western Hemisphere “in flagrant cases of wrongdoing or impotence,” arguing that America might be obliged to carry out “the exercise of an international police power.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt sends annual message to Congress

events.title: Trade agreements with the Dominican Republic
events.description: The United States signs a protocol with the Dominican Republic, thereby giving it control of the latter's customs and international in and mollifying European creditors. Though the Senate refuses to ratify this agreement, Roosevelt makes a temporary arrangement with the republic to undertake the newly envisioned “Roosevelt Corollary.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Trade agreements with the Dominican Republic

events.title: National Forest Service established
events.description: Roosevelt establishes the National Forest Service.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: National Forest Service established

events.title: Roosevelt inaugurated
events.description: Roosevelt is inaugurated for his first full term as President of the United States. Also sworn in is Vice President Charles W
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt inaugurated

events.title: National Monuments Act
events.description: Roosevelt signs the National Monuments Act, establishing the first eighteen national monuments, including Devils Tower, Muir Woods, and Mount Olympus.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: National Monuments Act

events.title: Hepburn Act
events.description: TR signs the Hepburn Act, which gives the Interstate Commerce Commission increased power to regulate railroad rates. Roosevelt's leadership is key for the passage of this act, as many observers claim the act would have not come out of the Senate without TR's advocacy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hepburn Act

events.title: Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act
events.description: Roosevelt signs the Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act. The legislation calls for both an honest statement of food content on labels and for federal inspection of all plants engaging in interstate commerce. The major impetus for these measures was The Jungle , the scathing report on meatpacking plants written by muckraking journalist Upton Sinclair, which TR personally read.
events.impact: Protected consumers from unsafe products

events.title: Immigration Act of 1907
events.description: TR signs the Immigration Act of 1907, which includes a provision allowing the President to restrict Japanese immigration. The issue had been a matter of great debate during TR's tenure, and Roosevelt proclaimed in his 1905 State of the Union address, that “probably a very large proportion, including most of the undesirable class [of immigrants], does not come here of its own initiative, but because of the activity of the agents of the great transportation companies, they wheedle and cajole many immigrants, often against their best interest, to come here.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Immigration Act of 1907

events.title: The Great White Fleet
events.description: Under Roosevelt's orders, the Great White Fleet (so named because of the boats' color) embarks on a voyage around the world from Hampton Roads, Virginia. The fleet returns triumphantly on February 22, 1909, having been enthusiastically welcomed at many ports and underscoring America's growing naval strength. The voyage would serve as Roosevelt's proudest accomplishment while in office. Great White Fleet Sails On December 16, 1907, President Theodore Roosevelt assembled the entire class of sixteen American battleships in Hampton Roads, Virginia, and launched them on a training cruise around the world. Labeled the “Great White Fleet,” in reference to the ships' new coat of white paint, the fleet visited Japan and China, passed through the Suez Canal, and called at several Mediterranean ports. Roosevelt scheduled the fleet to return to Hampton Roads on February 22, 1909, ten days before he left office. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Great White Fleet

events.title: National Commission for the Conservation of Natural Resources
events.description: Roosevelt establishes the National Commission for the Conservation of Natural Resources, headed by Gifford Pinchot.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Taft inaugurated
events.description: Roosevelt's administration ends with the inauguration of William Howard Taft as the twenty-seventh President. Roosevelt leaves on a yearlong African safari in order to avoid charges that he was attempting to run the White House from the shadows.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft inaugurated

events.title: Taft inaugurated
events.description: William Howard Taft takes the oath of office, becoming the twenty-seventh President of the United States. Taft had been handpicked by his predecessor, Theodore Roosevelt, and trusted to carry through Theodore Roosevelt's progressivism. Not surprisingly, Taft makes many references to his “distinguished predecessor” in his inaugural address. Nevertheless, a newfound chill had arisen between the two men, mirroring the frigid temperatures in the capital that day.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft inaugurated

events.title: Special session convenes
events.description: A special session of the United States Congress convenes to consider revision of the tariff. On March 16, Taft sends a special message to Congress urging prompt revision of the tariff.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session convenes

events.title: Taft's first message to Congress
events.description: Delivering a message to Congress, Taft proposes a two-percent tax on the net income of all corporations except banks, which he believes will make up for revenue lost by tariff reductions. He also proposes that Congress adopt a constitutional amendment that would permit the collection of personal federal income taxes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft's first message to Congress

events.title: Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act
events.description: Taft signs the Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act, which establishes a Tariff Board and reduces the tariff.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act

events.title: Taft tours the South, Southwest
events.description: President Taft begins a tour of the southern and western states of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft tours the South, Southwest

events.title: Supporting tariffs
events.description: While on a tour of the United States, Taft calls the Payne-Aldrich Act “the best” tariff bill ever passed by the Republican Party, leaving both Republican progressives and party regulars dismayed.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Supporting tariffs

events.title: Taft visits Porfirio DÌaz
events.description: Taft visits Mexican dictator Porfirio DÌaz at El Paso, Texas, and at Juarez, Mexico.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft visits Porfirio DÌaz

events.title: Warships to Nicaragua
events.description: Taft orders two U.S. warships to Nicaragua in response to the deaths of 500 revolutionaries, and two of their American advisors, at the hands of Nicaragua dictator José Santos Zelaya. The further threat of American force convinces Zelaya to retire on December 16.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Warships to Nicaragua

events.title: General Wood becomes Army Chief of Staff
events.description: Taft appoints General Leonard Wood as Chief of Staff of the Army. He also elevates circuit judge Horace H. Lurton to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: General Wood becomes Army Chief of Staff

events.title: Taft fires Gifford Pinchot
events.description: Taft fires Gifford Pinchot, head of the United States Forest Services, upon the release of a letter Pinchot had written to Senator Dolliver of Iowa on behalf of two of his employees implicated in the Glavis case. Pinchot was a leading conservationist and one of the most recognizable officials in the federal government.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Taft appoints Hughes to the Supreme Court
events.description: President Taft appoints Governor Charles E. Hughes of New York to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Hughes to the Supreme Court

events.title: Postal Savings Bank Act
events.description: Taft signs the Postal Savings Bank Act, which allowed one bank in each state, under federal supervision, to give two percent interest on accounts under $500.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Postal Savings Bank Act

events.title: Taft rejects joint honor
events.description: Taft rejects a proposed dinner, given by the National Conservation Congress, that would honor both himself and TR.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Taft supports Roosevelt
events.description: At the New York State Republican Convention in Saratoga, New York, Taft supports Roosevelt's choice for governor of New York, Henry Stimson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft supports Roosevelt

events.title: Taft appoints Willis Van Devanter
events.description: Taft appoints Willis Van Devanter to the Supreme Court to replace Justice William Moody.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Willis Van Devanter

events.title: Taft appoints Chief Justice
events.description: Taft appoints Associate Justice Edward White as Chief Justice of the Supreme Court; in January, Taft would also appoint Joseph R. Lamar to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Chief Justice

events.title: Investigating postal rates
events.description: Taft appoints a commission to investigate postal rates for newspapers and magazines; its report helps to convince Congress that a recent rate increase was justified.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Investigating postal rates

events.title: Mobilizing along Mexican border
events.description: Taft orders the mobilization of 20,000 American soldiers along the Mexican border after American ambassador to Mexico Henry Lane Wilson reports that the safety of Americans residing in Mexico may be endangered.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Mobilizing along Mexican border

events.title: Taft appoints Secretary of Interior
events.description: Taft appoints Walter Fisher, an ally of Gifford Pinchot, as Secretary of the Interior to replace Richard Ballinger, who resigned.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Secretary of Interior

events.title: Taft appoints Secretary of War
events.description: Taft appoints Henry Stimson secretary of war to replace Jacob Dickinson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Secretary of War

events.title: Canadian Tariff Reciprocity Agreement
events.description: Taft signs the Canadian Tariff Reciprocity Agreement.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Canadian Tariff Reciprocity Agreement

events.title: Arbitration with France and England
events.description: Taft signs general arbitration treaties with France and England. Roosevelt, along with his friend and ally Senator Henry Cabot Lodge, lead the campaign in opposition to the treaties.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arbitration with France and England

events.title: Taft vetoes tariff reductions
events.description: Taft vetoes tariff reductions on wool and woolen goods, arguing that the Tariff Board had not completed its investigation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft vetoes tariff reductions

events.title: Taft tours the western United States
events.description: Taft tours the western United States to drum up support for his arbitration treaties with England and France. In March 1912, the Senate will approve the treaties, which are rejected by Britain and France.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft tours the western United States

events.title: Taft calls for federal budget
events.description: Taft urges the adoption of an annual federal budget.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft calls for federal budget

events.title: President Taft nominates Mahlon Pitney
events.description: President Taft nominates Mahlon Pitney for a seat on the U.S. Supreme Court. Pitney is confirmed by the Senate and takes his oath on March 13.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Taft nominates Mahlon Pitney

events.title: Washington's first cherry trees
events.description: Taft plants the first of the cherry trees in Washington, D.C., given to the United States by Japan as a symbol of international friendship, along the Tidal Basin of Potomac Park.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington's first cherry trees

events.title: Creation of the Children's Bureau
events.description: Taft signs a bill authorizing the creation of the Children's Bureau in the Department of Commerce. The agency is charged with monitoring child welfare.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Creation of the Children's Bureau

events.title: Julia Lathrop heads the Children's Bureau
events.description: President Taft appoints Julia Lathrop head of the newly-created Children's Bureau. She is the highest ranking woman in the U.S. government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Julia Lathrop heads the Children's Bureau

events.title: Taft wins Republican nomination
events.description: Taft wins the Republican presidential nomination over Theodore Roosevelt. James Sherman is re-nominated for vice-president. The bitter primary campaign between TR and Taft featured a thorough discussion within the Republican Party on the issue of government regulation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft wins Republican nomination

events.title: Panama Canal Act
events.description: Taft signs the Panama Canal Act, which exempts American coastwise shipping from paying tolls when transiting the Panama Canal. Many Americans, as well as Britons, consider this a violation of the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty of 1901.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Taft vetoes literacy tests
events.description: Taft vetoes a bill calling for literacy tests for immigrants.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft vetoes literacy tests

events.title: Huerta overthrows Madero
events.description: General Victoriano Huerta overthrows Mexican President Madero. Taft refuses to intervene, despite the American public's calls for action.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Huerta overthrows Madero

events.title: Webb-Kenyon Interstate Liquor Act
events.description: Over Taft's veto, Congress passes the Webb-Kenyon Interstate Liquor Act, which prohibits the shipment of liquor into “dry” states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Webb-Kenyon Interstate Liquor Act

events.title: Wilson inaugurated
events.description: Woodrow Wilson is inaugurated as the twenty-eighth President of the United States. The Tafts leave Washington for a vacation in Augusta, Georgia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson inaugurated

events.title: Woodrow Wilson inaugurated
events.description: Woodrow Wilson is inaugurated as the twenty-eighth President of the United States. He proclaims it his duty “to cleanse, to reconsider, to restore, to correct the evil without impairing the good, to purify and humanize every process of our common life without weakening or sentimentalizing it.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Woodrow Wilson inaugurated

events.title: Republic of China recognized
events.description: President Wilson extends official recognition to the new Republic of China.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republic of China recognized

events.title: Foreign policy with Mexico
events.description: After considerable political instability in Mexico, following the assassination of President Francisco Madero, President Wilson declares the United States policy towards Mexico to be one of “watchful waiting.” Wilson refuses to recognize the new government of General Victoriano Huerta, who led the coup against Madero on February 22.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Foreign policy with Mexico

events.title: Underwood-Simmons Tariff Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Underwood-Simmons Tariff Act, considerably reducing rates set by previous Republican administrations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Underwood-Simmons Tariff Act

events.title: Federal Reserve Act
events.description: In an effort to safeguard America's financial institutions, the American economy, and the supply of U.S. currency, the Federal Reserve Act is signed into law. In contrast to the economies of Europe, the U.S. economy had functioned without the sophisticated management of banking ever since Andrew Jackson destroyed the Second Bank of the United States in 1830. The Federal Reserve Act created a Federal Reserve System, comprised of a Federal Reserve Board, twelve regional reserve banks, and the underpinnings of a smooth central banking system. Federal Reserve Act Signed On December 23, 1913, President Woodrow Wilson signed the Federal Reserve Act into law. The act created a Federal Reserve System, comprised of a Federal Reserve Board, twelve regional reserve banks, and the underpinnings of a smooth central banking system. [truncated]
events.impact: Stabilized the banking system

events.title: Tensions at Tampico
events.description: In the port of Tampico, Mexican officials detain several U.S. Marines from the U.S.S. Despite the their quick release and an expression of regret by President Victor Huerta, U.S. Admiral Henry T. Mayo demands that Mexican troops salute an American flag as a sign of contrition. President Huerta refuses the demanded salute on April 11; three days later President Wilson orders American warships to Tampico Bay.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Tensions at Tampico

events.title: Sanctioned force against Mexico
events.description: In order to “obtain from General Huerta and his adherents the fullest recognition of the rights and dignity of the United States,” President Wilson requests authorization from Congress to use force in Mexico. After some debate, both houses sanction such force on April 22.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Sanctioned force against Mexico

events.title: Resolving the Tampico controversy
events.description: President Wilson accepts the offer of arbitration presented by the “ABC Powers” of Argentina, Brazil, and Chile to resolve the Tampico controversy. The mediation proves unnecessary when Mexican President Huerta is forced to resign on July 15.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Resolving the Tampico controversy

events.title: Federal Trade Commission established
events.description: President Wilson signs legislation establishing the Federal Trade Commission, which is designed to regulate business conglomeration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal Trade Commission established

events.title: Literacy tests for immigrants approved
events.description: Congress approves a bill requiring literacy tests for all immigrants to the United States, although President Wilson vetoes the bill on January 28. Proponents of immigration restriction argue that the United States is allowing too many ill-qualified immigrants into the country, and justify their positions by appealing to religious, ethno-cultural, or racial prejudice.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Literacy tests for immigrants approved

events.title: William Jennings Bryan resigns
events.description: William Jennings Bryan resigns as secretary of state in protest over the Wilson administration's handling of the Lusitania sinking. Bryan thinks Wilson is acting too boldly and calls on him to take a more moderate approach, banning American travel on belligerents' ships. Wilson names Robert Lansing acting secretary of state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William Jennings Bryan resigns

events.title: Wilson appoints Louis B. Brandeis
events.description: Wilson appoints Louis B. Brandeis to the Supreme Court. He is the first Jewish justice in American history.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson appoints Louis B. Brandeis

events.title: Federal Farm Labor Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Federal Farm Labor Act, establishing a banking system for farmers to improve their holdings.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal Farm Labor Act

events.title: Adamson Eight-Hour Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Adamson Eight-Hour Act, mandating an eight-hour day standard for most railroad workers.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Woodrow Wilson reelected
events.description: Woodrow Wilson is reelected President of the United States by a 23-vote margin in the Electoral College. Wilson staves off stiff competition from Charles Evans Hughes, winning a 49.6 percent majority of the popular vote versus Hughes' 46.1 percent. Wilson runs on the slogan “He kept us out of War” despite the growing implausibility of U.S. neutrality in the Great War. The election hinged on Wilson's slim 4,000-vote majority in California, where Hughes' loss of support from Governor Hiram Johnson may have cost him the election. In congressional elections, the Democrats maintain a 53-42 majority in the Senate and a thin 216-210 majority in the House of Representatives.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Woodrow Wilson reelected

events.title: Wilson's dispatch to Europe
